title: English for Work and Career
desc: Practical business English: emails, meetings, interviews, presentations, negotiation, and CVs.

lessons.title: Professional Emails That Get Read
lessons.content:
Professional Emails

Clear emails save time and build credibility.

The structure

A good business email has five parts: (1) subject line, (2) greeting, (3) purpose, (4) request or action, (5) sign-off.

Subject line rules: 6-10 words. Specific, not vague.
❌ "Question"
✅ "Question about Tuesday's invoice — quick reply please"
❌ "Hi"
✅ "Proposal v2 attached — can you review by Friday?"

Common formulas

Opening: Hi [Name], / Hello [Name], / Dear [Name], (formal)
Purpose: I'm writing to... / I wanted to follow up on... / Thanks for your message about...
Request: Could you please...? / Would it be possible to...? / I'd appreciate it if you could...
Closing: Best regards, / Best, / Thanks, / Kind regards,

Full example:
Subject: Q3 budget review — meeting Thursday 3pm?

Hi Maria,

I'm writing to schedule our quarterly budget review. Could we meet Thursday at 3pm? I'll bring the updated forecast.

Please let me know if that works.

Best,
João

"The most valuable of all talents is that of never using two words when one will do."
— Thomas Jefferson (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: Which subject line is most effective for a professional email?
o: Proposal v2 attached — can you review by Friday?
quiz.exp: Effective subject lines are specific and action-oriented. They tell the recipient what the email is about and what is expected — saving time and increasing the chance of a timely reply.

lessons.title: Meeting English: Leading and Participating
lessons.content:
Meeting English

Speak with confidence in international meetings.

Opening a meeting

"Thanks for joining today." / "Let's get started." / "On the agenda we have three items." / "I'd like to begin with..."

Asking for opinions: "What do you think, Carlos?" / "Maria, do you have any thoughts on this?" / "Can we hear from the marketing team?"
Agreeing: "That makes sense." / "I see your point." / "I'd go further and say..."
Disagreeing politely: "I see it a little differently." / "I'd push back gently on that." / "That's a fair point, but have we considered...?"
Clarifying: "Just to make sure I understood — are you saying...?" / "Could you elaborate on that?"

Closing a meeting

"Let's recap the action items." / "Maria will send the report by Friday." / "We'll meet again next Tuesday." / "Thanks everyone — have a great day."

Cross-cultural tip: Anglophone business culture often values speaking up. If you wait to be invited, you may not get the floor. Practice: prepare 1-2 short comments before the meeting and find a moment to deliver them. Even "I'd just add that..." earns you a place at the table.

"Speak when you are angry and you will make the best speech you will ever regret."
— Ambrose Bierce (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) — preparation beats improvisation.

quiz.q: Which phrase politely signals disagreement in a business meeting?
o: I'd push back gently on that.
quiz.exp: Anglophone business culture favors hedged, indirect disagreement. "I'd push back gently" or "I see it a little differently" lets you challenge ideas without attacking the person — preserving the working relationship.

lessons.title: Job Interviews in English
lessons.content:
Job Interview English

Prepare for the questions you can predict.

The classics

Six questions appear in almost every English-language job interview:

1. Tell me about yourself. (90 seconds: present role → key skills → why this job)
2. Why do you want this job? (specific to the company and role)
3. What's your greatest strength? (one strength + concrete example)
4. What's a weakness? (real weakness + what you're doing about it)
5. Tell me about a time you faced a challenge. (use STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result)
6. Where do you see yourself in five years? (growth aligned with the company)

The STAR method

For behavioral questions: Situation (1 sentence) → Task (what you needed to do) → Action (what YOU did, not the team) → Result (with numbers if possible).

STAR example: "Last year our team was missing deadlines (S). I was asked to find out why (T). I interviewed each member, mapped the workflow, and identified that we had no shared task tracker (A). I implemented Trello and weekly 15-minute check-ins. Within two months, on-time delivery rose from 60% to 92% (R)."

"Success depends upon previous preparation, and without such preparation there is sure to be failure."
— Confucius (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: What does the STAR method help you do?
o: Structure answers to behavioral interview questions: Situation, Task, Action, Result
quiz.exp: STAR turns vague answers ("I'm a team player") into concrete, evaluable stories. Recruiters consistently rate STAR-structured answers higher because they reveal what you actually did and what happened.

lessons.title: Giving Presentations in English
lessons.content:
Presenting in English

Tell them what you'll say, say it, tell them what you said.

The classic structure

Three parts: opening (10%), body (80%), closing (10%).

Opening phrases: "Good morning everyone, thanks for being here." / "Today I'll cover three main points." / "By the end of this presentation, you'll know X, Y, and Z."
Signposting (the audience's GPS): "Let me start with..." / "Moving on to my second point..." / "That brings us to..." / "Before I conclude..." / "To summarize..."
Engaging the audience: "Has anyone here experienced...?" / "I'd love to hear your thoughts at the end." / "This data may surprise you."
Closing: "To wrap up, the three takeaways are..." / "I'm happy to take questions." / "Thank you for your attention."

Slide rules

1. One idea per slide. 2. Title that states the conclusion, not the topic. 3. 6×6 rule: max 6 lines, max 6 words per line. 4. Charts > tables for trends. 5. Numbers in big font when they matter. 6. Don't read your slides — speak to the audience, glance at slides.

Title that works: "Q3 sales rose 18% — driven by Mexico" (states the conclusion).
Title that doesn't: "Q3 Sales Results" (forces audience to figure it out).

"If you can't write your message in a sentence, you can't say it in an hour."
— Dianna Booher (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: Which slide title is most effective?
o: Q3 sales rose 18% — driven by Mexico
quiz.exp: A slide title that states the conclusion lets the audience absorb the point in one second. Topic-only titles ("Q3 Sales Results") force the audience to do interpretive work. State the message; let the slide back it up.

lessons.title: Negotiation Vocabulary
lessons.content:
Negotiation in English

Know the words; understand the moves.

Key concepts (Roger Fisher and William Ury, Getting to Yes, 1981)

BATNA — Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement. Your fallback if no deal. The stronger your BATNA, the stronger your position.
ZOPA — Zone of Possible Agreement. The overlap between what you'll accept and what they'll accept.
Anchor — the first number proposed. It strongly influences the final number.
Reservation price — your walk-away point.
Win-win — finding agreements both sides prefer over no deal.

Useful phrases:
• "What would it take to make this work?"
• "That's outside our budget — could you do X?"
• "Help me understand the reasoning behind that number."
• "I appreciate the offer, but I'd need to see..."
• "Is there flexibility on the timeline?"
• "Let me think about that and come back tomorrow."
• "If we agreed on X, could you offer Y?"

The anchor effect

Research (Tversky & Kahneman, 1974; Galinsky & Mussweiler, 2001) shows the first offer pulls the final price toward it. If you state your number first with a justification, you usually do better than letting the other side anchor.

Practical: "Based on three comparable contracts and the scope we've defined, I'd propose $X." The justification matters as much as the number.

"Let us never negotiate out of fear. But let us never fear to negotiate."
— John F. Kennedy, 1961 (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

o: Your Best Alternative to a Negotiated Agreement — what you'll do if no deal is reached
quiz.exp: Knowing your BATNA before negotiating tells you how much risk you can take and when to walk away. Fisher and Ury's central insight: a strong BATNA is the most reliable source of negotiating power.

lessons.title: Writing a CV and LinkedIn Profile in English
lessons.content:
CV and LinkedIn in English

Recruiters scan in 7-10 seconds — make them count.

CV structure

Standard sections: Header (name, contact, LinkedIn URL) → Summary (2-3 lines) → Experience (most recent first) → Education → Skills → Languages/Certifications.

Bullet points that work: Start with strong action verbs + quantified result.
❌ "Responsible for marketing."
✅ "Led email marketing campaigns generating 12,000 leads in 2024 (+34% YoY)."
❌ "Worked on customer service."
✅ "Reduced average response time from 8h to 90 minutes by redesigning the ticketing workflow."

Strong action verbs

Achieved, Led, Built, Designed, Launched, Reduced, Increased, Optimized, Negotiated, Mentored, Implemented, Analyzed, Delivered, Streamlined, Developed.

LinkedIn headline:
❌ "Marketing Professional"
✅ "Marketing Manager | B2B SaaS | Demand Gen, ABM, Lifecycle Marketing"
LinkedIn About: Write in first person. 3-5 short paragraphs. Lead with the value you bring, not your job title.

"You never get a second chance to make a first impression."
— Will Rogers (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: Which CV bullet point is strongest?
o: Led email marketing campaigns generating 12,000 leads in 2024 (+34% YoY)
quiz.exp: Strong bullets begin with an action verb and end with a measurable result. "Responsible for" describes a job description; "Led [thing] generating [number]" describes accomplishment.

lessons.title: Networking in English
lessons.sub: Small talk, elevator pitch, and follow-ups
lessons.content:
Networking

Most professional opportunities come through warm connections.

Small talk openers

"Is this your first time at this conference?" / "What brings you here?" / "Have you been to many talks today?" / "I noticed your badge says X — what does your team do?"

The elevator pitch (30 seconds):
1. Who you are ("I'm Renato, I run an EdTech platform in Brazil.")
2. What you do ("We bring high-quality, ideologically balanced education to adults who missed it.")
3. Who you serve ("Our users are mostly 30-50, working class, learning on their phones.")
4. What you want ("I'm here to find education researchers and potential partners in Latin America.")

Following up

Within 48 hours: a short LinkedIn message or email. "Hi [Name], great talking with you at [event] yesterday. I enjoyed our conversation about [specific topic]. I'd love to keep in touch — feel free to reach out if I can help on X."

The reciprocity rule (Robert Cialdini, Influence, 1984): If you offer something useful (an introduction, an article, a quick piece of help) before asking for anything, your network grows much faster. "What can I do for you?" beats "What can you do for me?"

"Your network is your net worth."
— Porter Gale (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: What is the most effective time to follow up after a networking conversation?
o: Never — wait for them to reach out
Within 48 hours, with a personalized message referencing what you discussed
quiz.exp: Follow-up within 48 hours catches the connection while it's fresh. Personalizing it (referencing the specific conversation topic) signals attention and turns a brief encounter into a relationship.

lessons.title: English for Reports and Memos
lessons.content:
Reports and Memos

Busy readers skim — write for skimming.

Inverted pyramid

Borrowed from journalism: put the conclusion first. The reader who only reads the headline still gets the point.

Memo structure:
• TL;DR (1-2 sentences with the recommendation)
• Background (what's the situation)
• Analysis (key facts and reasoning)
• Options (with trade-offs)
• Recommendation
• Next steps (who does what by when)

Writing rules (William Zinsser, On Writing Well; Strunk & White, Elements of Style)

1. Cut empty words: "In order to" → "To." "Due to the fact that" → "Because."
2. Use active voice: "We launched" beats "It was launched."
3. Specific beats vague: "$2.4M" beats "significant amount."
4. Short sentences. Average 15-20 words.
5. One idea per paragraph.

Before: "It is recommended that, in order to address the matter, the team should consider implementing a new tracking system." (22 words, passive)
After: "Recommendation: implement a new tracking system." (6 words, direct)

"Vigorous writing is concise. A sentence should contain no unnecessary words, a paragraph no unnecessary sentences."
— William Strunk Jr., 1918 (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: What is the principle of the "inverted pyramid" in business writing?
o: Put the conclusion first, then supporting details
Write the longest paragraphs at the end
quiz.exp: The inverted pyramid (from journalism) places the most important information first, followed by progressively less critical details. A reader who stops at the first paragraph still gets the main point — essential when readers are busy.

lessons.title: Cross-Cultural English: Working with Americans, British, and Others
lessons.content:
Cross-Cultural English

Same language, different cultural defaults.

Communication styles (Erin Meyer, The Culture Map, 2014)

Cultures vary on at least eight dimensions. Two important ones for English-speaking work:

Direct vs. indirect feedback:
• Dutch and German: very direct ("This proposal won't work because...")
• American: positive-framed direct ("Great work — one suggestion: ...")
• British: very indirect ("That's interesting — have you considered...?" often means "this is wrong")
• Brazilian: highly indirect, relational.

Decision-making:
• American: top-down, fast.
• German: bottom-up consensus, slower but durable.
• Japanese: nemawashi consensus before any meeting.

British understatement

British speakers often soften criticism. "Quite good" can mean "actually mediocre." "With the greatest respect" usually precedes serious disagreement. "That's a brave decision" can mean "that's reckless." Listen for hedges: "I'm not entirely sure that..." / "There may be one or two issues..."

The risk for Brazilians: Brazilian work culture often uses warmth and indirectness, then assumes urgency comes through tone. Americans miss the urgency. Solution: state deadlines explicitly. "I need this by Friday at 5pm Brazil time" beats "Could you maybe try to get this done soon?"

"Two countries separated by a common language."
— attributed to George Bernard Shaw (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46) — applies to American vs. British English, and to every cross-cultural pairing.

quiz.q: When a British colleague says "That's a brave decision," they often mean:
o: A polite warning that the decision looks reckless or risky
quiz.exp: British understatement often inverts surface meaning. "Brave" applied to a business decision is typically a soft warning. Listening for hedges and reading context is essential to working with British colleagues.

lessons.title: Career English Synthesis: Putting It All Together
lessons.sub: From CV to first day on the job
lessons.content:
Career English: Synthesis

Ten habits that compound over a career.

Ten habits

1. Read job ads weekly, even when not job hunting — you learn the vocabulary that matters in your field.
2. Keep a brag file — accomplishments, with numbers, kept in English. Updates your CV in 10 minutes.
3. Write one professional email a week in English, even to yourself. Maintains fluency.
4. Watch one industry talk a week (TED, podcasts, YouTube). Trains ear for native speed.
5. Use "can you check this for me?" with a native speaker friend or AI. Catches errors invisible to you.
6. Learn 5 new collocations per week ("raise concerns," "meet a deadline," "close a deal"). Native fluency comes from word combinations, not single words.
7. Practice the elevator pitch until it's automatic. Update it every 6 months.
8. Network deliberately — 1 new connection per month, with a follow-up.
9. Send the thank-you email after every interview. Most candidates don't.
10. Speak up early in meetings. The first comment is the hardest; subsequent ones are easier.

The compound effect

None of these habits is heroic. Done weekly for two years, they produce someone who is unmistakably professional in English. The career advantage is significant: research from McKinsey, Pew, and Brookings consistently finds large wage premiums for workers with strong English skills in non-Anglophone economies.

For Brazilians: The Brazilian Ministry of Education's English Olympiad and FGV's Cademi data show that proficient English correlates with 30-50% higher wages, even controlling for education and experience. The investment compounds.

"The limits of my language mean the limits of my world."
— Ludwig Wittgenstein, 1922 (Lei 9.610/98 Art. 46)

quiz.q: Why does learning collocations (word combinations) matter more than single words for fluency?
o: Native fluency depends on idiomatic word combinations ("raise concerns," "meet a deadline") more than on vocabulary size
quiz.exp: Researchers like Michael Lewis (The Lexical Approach, 1993) and corpus linguists since have shown that native speakers think in chunks. Knowing "raise" and "concerns" separately doesn't help if you don't know they pair naturally. Collocations are the fast lane to fluency.
